title: How Tier Lists Became the Internet's Favorite Way to Start an Argument
description: From fighting games to fast food to presidential candidates — tier lists have gone from niche gaming format to universal cultural phenomenon. Here's the history and why everyone's ranking everything.
lede: In 2026, you can find a tier list for literally anything: Costco items, US presidents, Taylor Swift albums, programming languages, types of cheese, Marvel characters, dating apps, and every video game character ever created. What started as a competitive gaming tool for ranking Street Fighter characters has become the internet's default format for structured disagreement — and it's only getting bigger.

faqs.a: S tier stands for 'Special' or 'Superb' — it originated in Japanese gaming culture where S rank was used above A as the highest grade. In tier lists, S tier means the absolute best of the best, above A tier.

faqs.q: Where did tier lists come from?
faqs.a: Tier lists originated in the fighting game community (FGC) in the early 2000s, where players ranked characters by competitive viability. The format spread to other gaming communities, then to YouTube and social media, and eventually to ranking anything and everything.

faqs.q: How do I make my own tier list?
faqs.a: Use a free online tier list maker — add items (text or images), then drag them into S/A/B/C/D/F tiers to rank them. Most tools let you save the result as an image to share on social media.

faqs.q: Why are tier lists so popular on social media?
faqs.a: Tier lists are engagement machines. Everyone has opinions, and a tier list makes those opinions visual and comparable. Disagreement drives comments ('How is X only B tier?!'), which drives algorithmic reach. They're easy to create, easy to consume, and impossible to agree on — perfect social media content.

sections.heading: From Street Fighter to Everything
paragraphs: The original tier lists were deadly serious. In the early 2000s, competitive Street Fighter players created rankings of every character based on frame data, matchup spreads, and tournament results. Characters in S tier dominated tournaments. Characters in F tier were unplayable at a competitive level. The tier list was a tool — not content.

Then YouTube happened. Gaming content creators realized that tier list videos generated massive engagement because viewers couldn't resist commenting about what the creator got wrong. A 'Ranking Every Pokemon' video could generate 10x the views of a standard gameplay video. The format was engagement bait before the term existed.

sections.heading: The Format That Works for Everything
paragraphs: Tier lists escaped gaming around 2019-2020 and colonized every corner of the internet. Food tier lists (ranking fast food chains, pizza toppings, Thanksgiving dishes) became a staple of food content creators. Music tier lists sparked stan wars. Movie tier lists started genuine film criticism debates. Even corporate Twitter accounts started posting tier lists to boost engagement.

The format works because it combines two things humans love: ranking things and arguing about rankings. A tier list is a structured opinion — it gives you enough information to agree or disagree without requiring a 2,000-word essay. It's the perfect unit of internet discourse.
Create your own with our Tier List Maker — drag-and-drop items into S/A/B/C/D/F tiers, use pre-made templates for gaming and pop culture, and export as a shareable image.

sections.heading: The Creator Economy's Cheat Code
paragraphs: For content creators, tier lists are algorithmic gold. They're cheap to produce (no special equipment needed), generate high engagement (comments arguing about placements), have built-in rewatch value (viewers pause to read every tier), and work on every platform — YouTube, TikTok, Twitter, Instagram, Reddit.

The tier list format has also become a legitimate content creation tool. Educators use tier lists to rank study strategies. Financial advisors rank investment accounts. Fitness influencers rank exercises. The format has been so thoroughly adopted that 'just make a tier list video' is now standard advice for new creators struggling to grow their audience.
